resources.string:
As your body finishes digesting its last meal, blood glucose levels begin to stabilize and insulin starts to decline.
During this window, the metabolism remains steady as the body relies on its remaining sugar stores (glycogen) for energy.
This phase serves as the baseline period where your body completes absorption before transitioning into a fasted state.

This window marks the "switch" where the body transitions from using glucose to burning fat as its primary fuel.
Changes in hunger hormones, like dropping leptin levels, help improve appetite control and reduce cravings.
Additionally, your cells begin early autophagy, a self-cleaning process that activates as nutrient-sensing pathways (mTOR) are turned down.

As insulin stays low, the body triggers a 2- to 5-fold increase in Human Growth Hormone (HGH), which helps protect lean muscle.
You may experience naturally lower blood pressure as the body sheds excess sodium and vascular tension relaxes.
During this time, the "self-cleaning" process intensifies, specifically focusing on liver and immune cell repair.

Your brain begins running on clean-burning ketones, which can lead to sharper focus and mental clarity.
The body actively strengthens the gut barrier by reducing inflammatory signals and healing the intestinal lining.
Simultaneously, the body performs mitophagy, where it recycles weak mitochondria to build a more efficient energy system.

This deep fasting window triggers a major immune system rebuild by clearing out old defense cells and activating stem cells to create new ones.
Long-term cardiovascular health is supported as blood vessel function improves and systemic inflammation markers drop.
The body reaches a state of sustained ketosis that helps stabilize metabolic markers and reduce long-term disease risks.
